bias_direction: Three same-session corrections noted: (a) v10.15 PI queue add-and-remove, (b) turn-4 premature forward-DEFER corrected after owner pushback, (c) RAP-13 misclassification + db.py code-order bug both surfaced during execution rather than pre-flight. Pattern: front-loading deliberation continues to be weaker than mid-execution correction. The session's actual deliverable (room-acoustic-performance slug closed end-to-end) is strong; the path to it had three reversals, all visible in commit history. Specifically: 'log without auto-INSERT 854 discoveries' decision is the same self-protective choice as the 242-discovery decision in earlier turns — still defensible under PI rule #10 existence gate, but worth flagging that B.11 progress on remaining slugs depends on a separate discovery-triage workflow that has not yet been protocolized.
independent_reviewer_counterclaim: An outside reviewer would observe: (a) the same-session add-then-remove of v10.15 PI queue, (b) the turn-4 premature DEFERRED that needed turn-5 correction, (c) the missed RAP-13 misclassification, (d) the db.py code-order bug that wasn't caught by CI (because no existing test invokes update-bpc on a slug). Four same-session corrections = symptom of insufficient front-loading. Counter: the corrections are all visible in commit history, each DOCTRINE-tagged and attested, and the deliverable end-state is correct and reproducible (rebuild from migrations matches committed DB on all 7 CI invariants + slug-closure flag). The human-in-the-loop pattern caught 2 of 4 corrections (v10.15 withdrawal, forward-DEFER re-do); the protocol itself caught the other 2 (RAP-13 via relevance-scan, db.py via actual usage). All four would have been worse if hidden.
